3. Semiclassical (WKB) Approximation

Define the local momentum as , and the local de Broglie wavelength as . We assume a time-independent potential. The time-independent Schrodinger equation takes on a nice form:

(17)

In general, the wavefunction can be written as an amplitude plus a phase

(18)

for real functions and , where represents the probability density, and is related to the probability current via .

Let , so that our ansatz looks much simpler:

(19)

This is still general, since and can be identified from the real and imaginary components of , respectively.

    We can know define what we mean by a “slowly changing potential”: the change in the potential at the level of the de Broglie wavelength must be much smaller than the kinetic energy.

  3. Near classical turning points, goes to zero, implying the de Broglie wavelegnth goes to infinity, and the slow variation conditions do not hold.

Suppose we have a potential like the left one in Figure 2 , where the classical allowed region is to the left of the forbidden region. For convenience, we rewrite the general ansatz for region I (allowed) and II (forbidden) :

(27)

Note that there is no issue with setting to the turning point, since the coefficients absorb everything.

Since the Schrodinger equation is second order, we expect each solution to depend on two unknown constants. Thus in principle we should be able to write and in terms of and , and vice versa.

Theorem 3 (Connection Formulas). Suppose we have a potential with a turning point at . Suppose that the classically allowed (I) and forbidden (II) regions are to the left and right of , respectively. Then the connection formula relates WKB solutions far to the left and far to the right of :

(28)

Suppose the two regions are swapped. The equations take the form:

(29)

Verbally, if there is only a decay exponential on one side, then the other side must be only a cosine. If there is only a sine on one side, the other side must be a growing exponential.

Proof

[TODO] The idea is to model the potential as linear near a turning point (call this region III) and solve it exactly, yielding airy functions. We can match their asymptotic expansions (far away from the turning point) with WKB solutions.

□

Remarks.

  • The origin of the arrows comes from the uncertainty in the WKB approximation, as we neglected the impact of the higher order terms in the expansion of . For example, consider the first arrow in Equation 28 If we know takes the form of a decaying exponential for , then we know there cannot be a growing exponential in the real solution (since the WKB approximation would be completely incorrect). Thus we may use the connection formula from a decay exponential to a cosine. But the other way around doesn’t work. If the WKB approximation yields just a cosine, there could still be a small sine component from the higher order terms, causing a growing exponential in the other region, so we cannot reverse the arrow. Similar logic applies to the second arrow in Equation 28.

  • Validity of connection formulas: Region III is defined by to what extent the neighborhood of the turning point is linear. We must have an overlap between regions III/I and III/II so that we can match terms as shown in the figure below. Consider writing

    (30)

    Let where . This yields . We estimate the size of region 3 as the regime where , i.e. This must be far from the turning point so that the WKB approximation applies. In summary we have,

    (31)

3.2. Tunneling

Set up a scattering problem and solve it using WKB approximation. On the right side we have

(35)

We can connect the sine into a decreasing exponential away from (the growing exponential is not physical [TODO] argument with connection formula is sus). We can use the connection formula to yield an incident and reflected wave. The incident part turns out to be

(36)

where

The tunneling probability is given by the ratio of their probability currents:

(37)
